We reveal by this experiment that ELLA can be a more applicable and scalable method than most of existing BNNs. 6 Related Work LA [38, 50, 14, 30, 22, 6, 7] locally approximates the Bayesian posterior with a Gaussian distribution, analogous to VI with Gaussian variationals [16, 1, 36, 52, 46] and SW AG [39]. LA can be applied to pretrained models effortlessly while the acquired posteriors are potentially restrictive (see Section 5.5). VI enjoys higher ﬂexibility yet relies on costly training; BayesAdapter [10] seems to be a remedy to the issue but its accessibility is still lower than LA. SWAG stores a series of SGD iterates to heuristically construct an approximate posterior and is empirically weaker than LA/LLA [6]. Though more expressive approaches like deep ensembles [32] and MCMC [55, 4, 63] can explicitly explore diverse posterior modes, they face limitations in efﬁciency and scalability. What’s more, it has been shown that LA/LLA can perform on par with or better than deep ensembles and cyclical MCMC on multiple benchmarks [6]. This may be attributed to the un-identiﬁed, complicated relationships between the parameters space and the function space of DNNs [59]. Also of note that LA can embrace deep ensembles to capture multiple posterior modes [13]. [9] introduces a general kernel approximation technique using neural networks. By contrast, we focus on leveraging kernel approximation to accelerate Laplace approximation. Thus, the focus of the two works is distinct. Indeed, this work shares a similar idea with the Sec 4.3 in [ 9] that LLA can be accelerated by kernel approximation. But, except for such an idea, this work differentiates from [9] in aspects like motivations, techniques, implementations, theoretical backgrounds, and applications. Our implementation, theoretical analysis, and some empirical ﬁndings are all novel. 7 Conclustion This paper proposes ELLA, a simple, effective, and reliable approach for Bayesian deep learning. ELLA addresses the unreliability issues of existing approximations to LLA and is implemented based on Nyström method. We offer theoretical guarantees for ELLA and perform extensive studies to testify its efﬁcacy and scalability. ELLA currently accounts for only the predictive, and extending it to estimate model evidence for model selection [38] deserves future investigation. Using model evidence to select the number of remaining eigenpairs Kfor ELLA is also viable. B How Does ELLA Address the Scalability Issues of Nyström Approximation Existing works like NeuralEF [9] highlight the scalability issues of the Nyström approximation when applied to NTKs, but they have been successfully addressed by ELLA, which reﬂects our technical novelty. On the one hand, the cost of eigendecomposing the M ×M matrix in our case is low considering that we set M <104. As shown in Figure 2(a)(b), M = 2000 can lead to reasonably small approximation errors for both the Nyström approximation and the resulting ELLA covariance. On the other hand, the prediction cost at test time is reduced by two innovations in this work. Firstly, looking at Equation (14), typical Nyström method ﬁrst computes Jˆθ(x,i)J⊤ ˆθ,˜X (i.e., evaluate the NTK similarities between (x,i) and all training data ˜X) and then multiplies the result with uk ∈RM to get the evaluation of k-th eigenfunction. So it needs to store Jˆθ,˜X ∈RM×P and compute M Jacobian-vector products (JVPs). But this work proposes to ﬁrst computevk = J⊤ ˆθ,˜Xuk,k = 1,...,K (we omit the scalar multiplier) and then obtain the evaluation of k-th eigenfunction by Jˆθ(x,i)vk. Namely, we only need to store Kvectors of size P and compute KJVPs. In most cases, M = 2000 and K = 20, so the beneﬁts of our technique are obvious. Secondly, we leverage forward-mode 17Table 4: Test NLL of ELLA varies w.r.t.M and K(M ≥K) on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-20 architecture. Further, fwAD enables the parallel evaluation of JVPs over all output dimensions, i.e., we can concurrently compute Jˆθ(x,i)vk,i = 1,...,C in one forward pass. These techniques make the evaluation of ELLA equal to performing Kforward passes under the scope of fwAD. This is similar to other BNNs that perform Sforward passes with different MC parameter samples to estimate the posterior predictive. C More Experimental Results C.1 Test NLL of ELLA Varies w.r.t. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper addresses the scalability and fidelity issues of existing Linearized Laplace Approximation (LLA) methods in Bayesian deep learning. It introduces an Accelerated LLA (ELLA) that uses a Nyström approximation of the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) to efficiently compute the predictive posterior. This approach avoids explicit computation of large GGN or Jacobian matrices, scales to modern architectures like Vision Transformers, and comes with theoretical guarantees on its approximation error. The paper also identifies and provides a solution (early stopping) for an overfitting issue in LLA on large datasets.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: The method, named ELLA (accElerated Linearized Laplace Approximation), reformulates the LLA predictive posterior through its connection to Neural Tangent Kernels (NTKs). To avoid the costly computation and inversion of the full NTK Gram matrix, it employs the Nyström method to find a low-rank approximation. This involves constructing a feature map based on the top-K eigenfunctions of the NTK, estimated from a subset of M data points. The implementation is made efficient by using forward-mode automatic differentiation (fwAD) to compute the required Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) without explicitly forming the Jacobian matrix.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on a 1D regression task and image classification benchmarks including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Robustness was tested on out-of-distribution datasets like CIFAR-10/ImageNet corruptions and SVHN. A variety of neural network architectures were used, including MLPs, ResNets of different depths, and Vision Transformers (ViT-B). ELLA was compared against baselines such as MAP, Mean-Field Variational Inference (MFVI-BF), and various LLA approximations (last-layer, diagonal, KFAC). Evaluation metrics included test accuracy, Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), and Expected Calibration Error (ECE).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The paper identifies that LLA and ELLA can suffer from an overfitting issue when applied to large datasets, where predictive uncertainty diminishes too rapidly, leading to increased NLL. The proposed solution is a practical heuristic (early stopping). The performance of ELLA is dependent on the quality of the initial MAP-trained model, as it only adjusts the covariance of the posterior, not the mean. The choice of hyperparameters M (number of Nyström samples) and K (number of eigenpairs) requires empirical tuning.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The authors suggest extending ELLA to estimate the model evidence, which is useful for model selection. A related direction is to use this model evidence to automatically select the hyperparameter K, the number of eigenpairs retained in the Nyström approximation.

Code from utils.py:

@torch.enable_grad()
def jac(model, xs, ys, num_classes, full=False, dtype=torch.float32):
	I = torch.eye(num_classes).to(xs.device)
	Js = []
	for i, (x, y) in enumerate(zip(xs, ys)):
		o = model(x.unsqueeze(0))
		model.zero_grad()
		if full:
			for j in range(num_classes):
				o.backward(I[j].view(1, -1), retain_graph = False
					if j == num_classes - 1 else True)
				g = torch.cat([p.grad.flatten() for p in model.parameters()])
				Js.append(g)
				model.zero_grad()
		else:
			grad_in = I[y.item()].view(1, -1)
			o.backward(grad_in)
			g = torch.cat([p.grad.flatten() for p in model.parameters()])
			Js.append(g)
	return torch.stack(Js)

def build_dual_params_list(model, params, x_subsample, y_subsample, num_classes=10, random=True, K=20, args=None, num_batches=1, verbose=True):
	if args is not None:
		random = args.random
		num_classes = args.num_classes
		K = args.K
	indices = torch.empty_like(y_subsample).random_(num_classes).long() if random else y_subsample
	if num_batches == 1:
		Js = jac(model, x_subsample, indices, num_classes)
		mat = Js @ Js.T
	else:
		mat = torch.empty(x_subsample.shape[0], x_subsample.shape[0], device=x_subsample.device)
		s = x_subsample.shape[0] // num_batches
		for i in tqdm(range(0, x_subsample.shape[0], s), desc='Doing matmul', total=num_batches):
			ii = min(i + s, x_subsample.shape[0])
			Js_b = jac(model, x_subsample[i:ii], indices[i:ii], num_classes)

			for j in range(0, x_subsample.shape[0], s):
				jj = min(j + s, x_subsample.shape[0])
				Js_b2 = jac(model, x_subsample[j:jj], indices[j:jj], num_classes)
				mat[i:ii, j:jj] = Js_b @ Js_b2.T
				
		del Js_b, Js_b2

	p, q = psd_safe_eigen(mat)
	p = p[range(-1, -(K+1), -1)]
	q = q[:, range(-1, -(K+1), -1)]
	tmp = q.div(p.sqrt())
	p = (p / x_subsample.shape[0])

	if num_batches == 1:
		V = Js.T @ tmp
	else:
		V = torch.zeros(count_parameters(model), K, device=tmp.device)
		s = x_subsample.shape[0] // num_batches
		for i in tqdm(range(0, x_subsample.shape[0], s), desc='Doing matmul', total=num_batches):
			ii = min(i + s, x_subsample.shape[0])
			Js_b = jac(model, x_subsample[i:ii], indices[i:ii], num_classes)
			V += Js_b.T @ tmp[i:ii]
		del Js_b

	if verbose:
	   print('eigenvalues: ', p)

	dual_params_list = []
	for item in V.T:
		dual_params = {}
		start = 0
		for name, param in params.items():
			dual_params[name] = item[start:start+param.numel()].view_as(param)
			start += param.numel()
		dual_params_list.append(dual_params)
	return dual_params_list

def psd_safe_eigen(K):
	Kprime = K.clone()
	jitter = 0
	jitter_new = None
	while True:
		p, q = torch.linalg.eigh(Kprime)
		if (p > 0).all():
			if jitter_new is not None:
				warnings.warn(
					f"K not p.d., added jitter of {jitter_new} to the diagonal",
					RuntimeWarning,
				)
			return p, q
		else:
			if jitter == 0:
				jitter_new = 1e-10
			else:
				jitter_new = jitter * 10
		Kprime.diagonal().add_(jitter_new - jitter)
		jitter = jitter_new

@torch.no_grad()
def Psi_raw(model, params, dual_params_list, x_batch, return_output=False):
	with fwAD.dual_level():
		Jvs = []
		for dual_params in dual_params_list:
			for name, param in params.items():
				module, name_p = find_module_by_name(model, name)
				delattr(module, name_p)
				setattr(module, name_p, fwAD.make_dual(param, dual_params[name]))
			output, Jv = fwAD.unpack_dual(model(x_batch))
			Jvs.append(Jv)
	Jvs = torch.stack(Jvs, -1)
	if return_output:
		return Jvs, output
	else:
		return Jvs

Code from main.py:

def main():
    # ... (argument parsing and setup)
	args.random = not args.not_random

    x_subsample, y_subsample = subsample(train_loader_noaug, args.num_classes,
											 args.M, args.balanced,
											 device, verbose=False)
    dual_params_list = build_dual_params_list(model, params, x_subsample, y_subsample, args=args, num_batches=args.I)

    Psi = partial(Psi_raw, model, params, dual_params_list)

    cov = torch.zeros(args.K, args.K).cuda(non_blocking=True)
    for i, (x, y) in enumerate(train_loader_noaug):
        x, y = x.to(device, non_blocking=True), y.to(device, non_blocking=True)
        Psi_x, logits = Psi(x, return_output=True)
        prob = logits.softmax(-1)
        Delta_x = prob.diag_embed() - prob[:, :, None] * prob[:, None, :]
        cov += torch.einsum('bok,boj,bjl->kl', Psi_x, Delta_x, Psi_x)
    # ... (add prior, invert cov, and test)

def ella_test(test_loader, model, device, args, Psi, cov_inv, verbose=True, return_more=False):
	t0 = time.time()
	targets, confidences, predictions = [], [], []
	loss, acc, num_data = 0, 0, 0
	with torch.no_grad():
		for x, y in test_loader:
			x, y = x.to(device, non_blocking=True), y.to(device, non_blocking=True)
			Psi_x, y_pred = Psi(x, return_output=True)
			F_var = Psi_x @ cov_inv.unsqueeze(0) @ Psi_x.permute(0, 2, 1)
			F_var_L = psd_safe_cholesky(F_var)
			F_samples = (F_var_L @ torch.randn(F_var.shape[0], F_var.shape[1], args.num_samples_eval,
				device=F_var.device)).permute(2, 0, 1) * args.ntk_std_scale + y_pred
			prob = F_samples.softmax(-1).mean(0)

			loss += F.cross_entropy(prob.log(), y).item() * x.shape[0]
			conf, pred = torch.max(prob, 1)
			acc += (pred == y).float().sum().item()
			num_data += x.shape[0]
    # ... (compute metrics and print)
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info: The method employs the Nyström technique to approximate the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) Gram matrix. This is implemented in the `build_dual_params_list` function, which first computes the Jacobians (`jac`) for a subset of `M` data points to form an empirical NTK matrix (`mat = Js @ Js.T`). Then, it performs an eigendecomposition (`psd_safe_eigen`) on this matrix to find the top `K` eigenfunctions, which form a basis `V`. This basis is then used to define a feature map, `Psi_raw`, which efficiently computes Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) using forward-mode automatic differentiation (fwAD), avoiding the explicit construction of the Jacobian. The covariance matrix `cov` is accumulated over the entire training dataset using this feature map. Predictions are made in `ella_test` by computing the predictive variance `F_var` using the feature map of test inputs and the inverse of the covariance matrix. Key hyperparameters from the argument parser include `K` (number of eigenfunctions, default 20), `M` (number of data points for Nyström approximation, default 100), `sigma2` (prior variance, default 0.1), and `num-samples-eval` (number of Monte Carlo samples for prediction, default 512).

new_method.method:
{"Motivation": "Modern Meta-Continual Learning (Meta-CL) is split between two paradigms: first-order streaming methods like DGC and CSVRG, which are computationally efficient but geometrically naive, and offline second-order methods like ELLA and Laplace Redux, which capture rich geometric information but cannot incrementally fuse knowledge from a stream of tasks. This creates a critical gap for true lifelong learning systems that require both efficiency and rapid, geometrically-informed adaptation. The proposed research, Streaming Orthogonal Curvature Aggregation (SOCA), directly bridges this gap. SOCA introduces a novel framework to build and maintain a persistent, low-rank, global 'meta-curvature' model in a fully online manner. This model acts as a learned geometric prior on the task distribution, serving a dual role: it regularizes updates to remain within a shared low-loss subspace, preventing catastrophic forgetting, and simultaneously acts as a meta-learned pre-conditioner to accelerate few-shot adaptation to new tasks by identifying and amplifying learning along truly novel geometric directions.","Methodology": "SOCA advances beyond simple additive updates or replay-based methods by introducing a principled orthogonal fusion mechanism that explicitly disentangles new information from existing knowledge, ensuring the global model remains compact, expressive, and stable. The methodology unfolds in three key stages:
1. **Task-Local Curvature Estimation:** For each incoming task 't', we leverage the ELLA framework to compute an efficient, low-rank Hessian approximation, H_t ≈ V_t * Λ_t * V_t^T. This step uses a Nyström approximation of the Neural Tangent Kernel to extract the task's most salient curvature directions (its eigenbasis V_t) without forming the full Hessian.
2. **Streaming Orthogonal Curvature Fusion (Core Novelty):** This stage replaces naive additive fusion with a more stable, geometrically-grounded update. Given the current global curvature H_global = U_global * S_global * U_global^T, the update proceeds as follows:
   a) **Orthogonal Decomposition:** Project the new task's basis V_t onto the existing global basis U_global to isolate the novel geometric information orthogonal to the learned subspace: V_t_perp = V_t - U_global * (U_global^T * V_t).
   b) **Subspace Augmentation & Re-optimization:** Orthonormalize V_t_perp and augment the global basis to form U_aug = [U_global, V_t_perp_ortho]. A new, small subspace Hessian H_sub is formed by projecting a weighted average of the old global and new task Hessians onto this augmented basis: H_sub = U_aug^T * (α*H_global + (1-α)*H_t) * U_aug, where α is a forgetting factor. This update is performed on a small (K_global + K_task) x (K_global + K_task) matrix, ensuring efficiency.
   c) **Principled Truncation:** An eigendecomposition of the small H_sub matrix yields an updated global basis and eigenvalues. The new global model (U_global', S_global') is formed by selecting the top K_global eigenpairs, which guarantees bounded memory and computational complexity for the meta-model, irrespective of the number of tasks seen.
3. **Dual-Use Meta-Curvature for Stability and Plasticity:** The updated global model H_global' guides learning for subsequent tasks:
   a) **(Stability) Subspace Regularization:** A quadratic penalty L_reg = λ(θ - θ*)^T * H_global' * (θ - θ*) constrains parameter updates within the learned low-loss subspace of past tasks, explicitly preventing catastrophic forgetting.
   b) **(Plasticity) Meta-Preconditioning:** For new tasks, the inverse, (H_global')^-1, serves as a pre-conditioner for a quasi-Newton optimizer. This reshapes the loss landscape, damping updates in well-learned directions (large eigenvalues) and accelerating learning in novel, orthogonal directions (small eigenvalues), enabling rapid, meta-learned adaptation.","Expected impact": "1. **Unification of Streaming and Geometric CL:** SOCA will be the first framework to successfully unify the efficiency of streaming first-order methods with the geometric insight of second-order methods, creating a new class of practical and powerful lifelong learning algorithms.
2. **Principled Stability-Plasticity Management:** The orthogonal decomposition provides a formal mechanism to disentangle novel from existing knowledge. This allows the system to protect consolidated knowledge while remaining highly adaptive to new information, moving beyond heuristic or replay-dependent solutions to the stability-plasticity dilemma.
3. **Guaranteed Bounded Complexity for Lifelong Learning:** By maintaining a fixed-rank global curvature model through principled truncation, SOCA ensures that its memory and per-task computational overhead remain constant. This makes it a truly scalable solution for systems designed to learn continuously from infinite data streams.
4. **Enhanced Forward and Backward Transfer:** By learning a compact, non-redundant basis for the shared structure across tasks, SOCA is expected to yield superior knowledge transfer. The pre-conditioning mechanism will improve forward transfer (faster learning on new tasks), while the subspace regularization will improve backward transfer (less forgetting of old tasks)."}
